summary: Gruber breaks down Apple's latest sweeping but convoluted policy changes for EU App Store compliance with the Digital Markets Act. The changes include a new tiered fee structure, replacement of the per-download Core Technology Fee with a percentage-based Core Technology Commission, and new freedoms for developers to link to external payment options. Gruber argues the complexity is a natural consequence of a byzantine law producing byzantine compliance. He notes the changes create winners and losers: small developers with free apps benefit from dropping the CTF, but mega-apps like Instagram that sell no digital goods will again pay nothing. He speculates that the new fee math might imply EU developers get a better 80/20 split, which would only make sense if Apple plans to reduce commissions worldwide.
key_points: Apple is replacing the €0.50-per-download Core Technology Fee with a 5% Core Technology Commission on digital goods, eliminating the penalty that hurt small developers with popular free apps
The new fee structure is so convoluted that seemingly no one can definitively calculate what apps using alternative payments will actually owe Apple
Apps distributed outside the App Store only pay the 5% CTC, but there is no free lunch — every iOS app selling digital content must pay something
Apple introduced a 'Tier 1' reduced store services option that strips major App Store features, seemingly designed to justify that those features are worth 8% of commission
The new terms could be read to imply EU developers on IAP now pay only 20%, which would only make sense if Apple plans to cut commissions worldwide
money_quotes: It's a natural consequence that an overly complicated law (the DMA) has resulted in an ever-more-complicated set of guidelines and policies (from Apple): byzantine compliance with a byzantine law.
Even if you understand it enough to just want to express anger at Apple for spiteful compliance and greed, it's hard to sum up why you're angry in a succinct headline or tweet.
Feel free to blame Apple as much as you want for spiteful compliance (especially when it comes to payments made on the web, from links in apps), but part of this is on the European Commission.
But there is no free lunch — iOS apps and games distributed outside the App Store that require a purchase, or offer digital content for sale, must pay the 5% CTC.

That Apple should collect no commission or fees from apps distributed outside the App Store, nor any commission or fees from apps in the App Store that offer their own payment processing — and, thus, that Apple should set their own IAP commission accordingly, as something akin to Stripe or PayPal, in the single-digit percentage range. That's obviously not in Apple's interest. But it's also not what the European Commission has suggested the DMA demands.

That'd be really weird, insofar as it would mean that developers in the EU get an 80/20 split for App Store distribution + IAP, but apps everywhere else in the world still get 70/30 for the same thing.
key_insight: The DMA's regulatory complexity has produced compliance rules so convoluted that neither developers nor commentators can clearly determine what anyone actually owes, which serves neither Apple's nor developers' interests.
